ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit pie — apple pie (sweet pastry with spiced apple filling)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for apple pie as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared pastry dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical slice of apple pie weighs approximately 125 g, representing roughly one-eighth of a standard 9-inch pie.

nutrient_source.title: Apple pie, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 18302)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for apple pie (cr_id F11110003). HPB values used as primary where available; USDA SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamins, and phosphorus. Energy 281 kcal, protein 4.47 g, fat 13.17 g, carbohydrate 38.58 g, sugar 11.05 g, sodium 184 mg, dietary fibre 2.0 g, calcium 15 mg, iron 1.64 mg are taken directly from HPB source (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat 4.67 g per USDA SR Legacy FDC 18302.

gi_source.title: Apple pie — Glycemic Index entry, University of Sydney GI Database (Foster-Powell et al., 2002; Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 36 (low) for apple pie is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 38.58 × 1.25) / 100 = 17.4 per 125 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sugar content (11.05 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention; sodium at 184 mg/100 g is borderline elevated; saturated fat (4.67 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit apple pie to occasional small portions (≤80 g) and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-sugar foods, as the combined sugar and saturated fat load can exacerbate blood pressure through insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total carbohydrate 38.58 g/100 g with only 2.0 g dietary fibre/100 g; sugar 11.05 g/100 g; refined pastry flour drives rapid glucose absorption despite a low measured GI of 36, resulting in a glycemic load of 17.4 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat apple pie as an occasional indulgence only, strictly limiting portion size to ≤80 g, pairing with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and accounting for the full carbohydrate load in their meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.67 g/100 g and trans fat 0.1 g/100 g from butter-based pastry crust raise LDL cholesterol; high refined carbohydrate and sugar load contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit apple pie consumption due to its saturated fat and trans fat content from butter pastry, which can raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherogenesis; substituting butter with unsaturated fat alternatives would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.67 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from butter, combined with refined sugar (11.05 g/100 g), promote pro-inflammatory mediators relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise apple pie consumption as the butter-derived saturated fat and refined sugar promote systemic inflammation; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil-based pastry and reducing added sugar would improve suitability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple pie is a classic Western baked dessert consisting of a spiced apple filling — typically made from sliced apples, sugar, cinnamon, and sometimes nutmeg — encased in a buttery shortcrust or flaky pastry shell. The dish is baked until the crust is golden and the filling is tender and fragrant. It is one of the most iconic desserts in British and American culinary tradition, commonly served warm with vanilla ice cream, whipped cream, or custard. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 281 kcal, 4.47 g protein, 13.17 g fat (of which 4.67 g is saturated), and 38.58 g carbohydrate with 11.05 g sugar and 2.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 184 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is 36 (low), but the glycemic load per standard 125 g serving is 17.4, making portion control important for glycaemic management. The refined pastry crust is the primary driver of saturated fat and refined carbohydrate content. Apple pie is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension in regular or large portions, and its saturated fat and trans fat content from butter make it a moderate concern for cardiovascular health. It is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of apple pie?
Is apple pie high in sugar?
What is the glycemic index of apple pie?
Is apple pie suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in apple pie?
What allergens are in apple pie?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11110003, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 18302) for apple pie, an exact match. GI value of 36 confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2008). All sources are approved primary sources.
notes: Saturated fat (4.67 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (5.73 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.89 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol, potassium, vitamins B1/B2/B3/B6, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water content are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 18302 (apple pie, commercially prepared) as these fields are not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these values are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy entry for apple pie. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g per USDA SR Legacy FDC 18302 (confirmed zero). Trans fat of 0.1 g/100 g is estimated from butter content in the pastry crust consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. Apple pie is a Western dish of British and American origin; no mandatory local-language names apply under Project Veritas multilingual rules (Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine rules do not apply). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (36 × 38.58 × 1.25) / 100 = 17.4 for a 125 g serving. The input record listed GI source as 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; GI provenance has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as defensible ontology anchors for apple pie as a fruit-filled baked pastry.
